text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 08 October 2015 

Dr Amelia Hart
Riverside Medical Centre

12 Riverside Way, Southwark, London SE1 2AB
Dear Dr Amelia Hart
Mr Michael Turner, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K8374921 NHS No. 7492036158
Flat 4, 28 Mill Quay, London E14 9GX 

I conducted a scheduled telephone review with the above patient today, coordinated to coincide with his onshore period as he works offshore on a rotation. He reports ongoing generalised epilepsy and remains on his current antiepileptic regimen. Since our last contact three months ago, he has adhered to treatment and occupational safety guidance when offshore, including ensuring colleagues are aware of his condition and maintaining adequate rest when shift patterns allow.

He describes a recent fluctuation in seizure control around the transition from night to day shifts. Specifically, he reports two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week. These events were brief, without injury, and were witnessed onshore by his partner. He denies convulsive episodes, tongue biting, or incontinence. He notes increased fatigue and some low mood following the episodes but no new neurological symptoms, headaches, or visual disturbance.

He has generalised epilepsy. Current medication (patient-reported): Valproate 500 mg b.d., Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d., and Lamotrigine 100 mg b.d. He reports good tolerability overall with mild sedation in the evenings. No rash, no gastrointestinal upset, and no weight change of note. He does not drive and continues to follow DVLA guidance.

We discussed seizure precipitants relevant to his offshore work, including sleep disruption and variable meal times. He has agreed to maintain a detailed seizure and sleep diary over the next 6–8 weeks across both offshore and onshore rotations. Occupational health support remains in place; he continues to avoid lone work at height and adheres to site safety protocols.

Plan:
- Continue current antiepileptic medication unchanged pending review of the seizure/sleep diary and correlation with shift patterns.
- Arrange routine bloods (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) via his GP while onshore in the coming week; results to be copied to clinic.
- Book an EEG during his next onshore week to assess current background and generalised spike–wave burden.
- Follow-up: telephone review in 8 weeks timed to his onshore period, or sooner if he experiences any convulsive seizures, clusters, or injuries.

I will update you following the EEG and diary review. Please let me know if there are any concerns from the primary care perspective in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week
two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter reports specific seizure counts within a defined recent period: "two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week." Total seizures in the last week = 2 + 3 = 5. No other frequencies provided that would supersede this. Therefore, normalize to '5 per week'.
seizure_frequency_number: two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week
reference: two absence seizures and three petit mal in last week
